acceptance_targets: [CRAFT_REWARD_MEAL_TIMELINE] Reading snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:25; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:27; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:109; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:243; and snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:291, a cold reader can state exactly how many reward containers Tomas gets that day, when each one is opened, and why the evening meal is not the already-open noon container; no passage describes an already-opened container as newly sealed or newly steaming.

[CRAFT_SPATIAL_LEGIBILITY_FLOORS] A cold reader can read snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:177; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:181; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:205; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:243; and snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:249 and describe, without importing outside context, Noor's path from the cafeteria to Priya to Marcus and back down to the cafeteria, with each stop assigned to a clear and consistent floor/location.

findings.acceptance_test: Reading snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:25; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:27; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:109; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:243; and snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:291, a cold reader can state exactly how many reward containers Tomas gets that day, when each one is opened, and why the evening meal is not the already-open noon container; no passage describes an already-opened container as newly sealed or newly steaming.
findings.problem: The Tomas reward-meal plant breaks object-state control at the exact point it is supposed to cleanly establish his privileged channel. A single thermal container is introduced in the morning, opened and eaten just before noon, then reappears hours later as "the same sealed thermal container" still steaming. That reads as a continuity error instead of a precise setup for the off-ledger meal system chapter 15 depends on.
findings.rewrite_direction: Repair the reward-meal thread at snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:25; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:27; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:109; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:243; and snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:291. Either make the noon container the only public reveal and cut the later "same sealed" restatement, or explicitly stage multiple containers / a visible stash so the reader can track when Tomas receives each meal, when each is opened, and why the later warm meal is a fresh privilege rather than the already-open lunch.

findings.acceptance_test: A cold reader can read snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:177; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:181; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:205; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:243; and snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:249 and describe, without importing outside context, Noor's path from the cafeteria to Priya to Marcus and back down to the cafeteria, with each stop assigned to a clear and consistent floor/location.
findings.problem: The chapter blurs floor-to-floor geography right when Lena's move-downstairs order becomes the scene's operational hinge. After Noor carries food "back upstairs," she later seems to move straight from the cafeteria doorway to Priya at the "server-room corridor" and then to Marcus, after which she tells him they still have to go downstairs. The reader can infer omitted movement, but the local floor map is not concrete enough to cleanly place Priya, Marcus, the cafeteria, and the server-room access in relation to one another.
findings.rewrite_direction: Tighten the movement logic at snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:67; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:177; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:181; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:205; snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:243; and snapshots/cycle_03/chapters/chapter_14.md:249. Add one or two explicit anchors for stair movement and floor location, and clarify whether Priya is working near an upstairs station pulling server-room data or physically near the first-floor server-room corridor before Noor returns to Marcus and brings him down.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if 'swallow' appears no more than once in the chapter and Min's death uses a different physiological marker. Fail if the word appears 3+ times.

evidence.excerpt: 3670: He swallows once. "If they've got bars today, don't tell me unless you stole one."

3732: Noor gives him the bowl. He takes it with both hands because one is not enough anymore. His fingers are puffy at the knuckles. Some of the rice spills back into his lap before he gets the angle right. He eats fast at first, then slows when the first swallow lands badly. His throat works. He swallows again by force.
3750: "Everything's useful late." He shoves another bite into his mouth, chews twice, stops, and has to swallow it in pieces. "Sorry. No. I don't know if it's useful."

3792: At first Min tries to push herself up. One arm answers. The other stays folded under her like it belongs to somebody else. Her legs bicycle once against the floor, soles scraping concrete. A thread of blood works out of her right ear. Her left eye blinks. The right one stares at nothing. Her mouth opens and closes around two empty swallows.

3671: 
3672: "Go back to sleep."
3673: 
3674: "I wasn't sleeping." He coughs into his wrist, turns away from the sound of it, and shuts his eye again. "Just get yours first."
3675: 
[...]
3787: 
3788: "Hall," Lena says, without turning from the terminal.
3789: 
3790: They drag Min into the corridor and leave her on her side where Noor can still see past the cafeteria door. She lands three feet from Kwame's blood smear. One of her shoes twists halfway off and stays there.
3791: 
3792: At first Min tries to push herself up. One arm answers. The other stays folded under her like it belongs to somebody else. Her legs bicycle once against the floor, soles scraping concrete. A thread of blood works out of her right ear. Her left eye blinks. The right one stares at nothing. Her mouth opens and closes around two empty swallows.

findings.problem: 'Swallow' appears four times across three characters — Kwame (3670), Marcus eating with difficulty (3732, 3750), and Min dying (3792, 'two empty swallows'). The repetition flattens what should be distinct physical experiences into the same default.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3670-3792, keep one instance (3732, where swallowing difficulty is most embedded in Marcus's eating scene). At :3670, replace 'He swallows once' with a different physical beat. At :3750, replace 'has to swallow it in pieces' with a different eating-difficulty detail. At :3792 (Min's death), replace 'two empty swallows' with a different dying-body detail — throat click, chest hitch, airway sound.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if 'shift' appears no more than once in the chapter. Fail if it appears 3+ times.

evidence.excerpt: 3682: Lena moves the thermal container to the far end of the counter without comment and writes something in the pocket notebook she keeps for food counts and watch rotations. Rodney works the doorway with a length of galvanized pipe. Eleven days ago he was the engineer who said excuse me in hallways and watched reinforcement-learning videos at lunch. He still says excuse me now when he shifts past somebody in line, pipe tucked against his leg like part of the apology. Elise stands near the reserve box with her own pipe leaning against the wall behind her. One of the other enforcers breaks down the water cases. Another drags the empty delivery crates back toward the loading dock. They all chew while they work.

3688: Lena uses the digital kitchen scale from the microwave station. She sets a bowl down and taps zero. She adds rice, zeros it again, spoons out a third of a can, and sets down a water cup. She does not hurry and does not vary. If a can opens light, she writes it down. If somebody reaches before she is done, Rodney shifts the pipe. The line learns the pace and holds it.

3716: Two enforcers drag him by the ankles. His scalp leaves a red crescent that smears into a streak. One shoe catches the cafeteria threshold and stays behind for a second before jerking loose. The breathing keeps going until they reach the corridor. Then there is a hollow knock against concrete, one last spill of air, and nothing.

3762: By late morning the line is gone, but nobody strays far from where food lives. People drift in and out on false errands, checking the water cases, checking whether another can has appeared, checking whether checking can be disguised as movement. The reserve box sits on the counter where everyone can see it. The SUSTAIN wrappers make a mute beige wall. Every time the cardboard shifts, heads lift.

rewrite_direction.excerpt: 3688: Lena uses the digital kitchen scale from the microwave station. She sets a bowl down and taps zero. She adds rice, zeros it again, spoons out a third of a can, and sets down a water cup. She does not hurry and does not vary. If a can opens light, she writes it down. If somebody reaches before she is done, Rodney shifts the pipe. The line learns the pace and holds it.

findings.problem: 'Shift' appears three times (3682, 3688 'Rodney shifts the pipe,' 3762 'the cardboard shifts') and 'for a second' once (3716) — four instances of default construction in the chapter.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3688, replace 'Rodney shifts the pipe' with 'Rodney adjusts the pipe' or 'Rodney moves the pipe forward.' At :3762, replace 'the cardboard shifts' with 'the cardboard rustles' or 'the stack settles.' Keep one 'shift' instance at :3682 if it is the strongest.

findings.acceptance_test: 'corridor' appears no more than 6 times in chapter 14.
findings.evidence: 'corridor' appears 14 times in Ch14 (L3656-3958, ~302 lines). Manuscript-wide total: 159 occurrences across 18 chapters.
findings.problem: The word 'corridor' appears 159 times in the manuscript (one every ~15 prose lines). Ch14 is tied for the worst chapter with 14 occurrences (~one per 21 lines). For an image appearing 20+ times, the target is retaining at most 8-10 manuscript-wide. At 159, approximately 90% need replacement or cutting.
findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch14, replace at least 8 of the 14 'corridor' instances with alternatives: hallway, hall, passage, the stretch between, the east run, or restructure sentences to avoid the spatial noun entirely.

findings.acceptance_test: The character name uses one consistent spelling/encoding in every occurrence.
findings.evidence: Chapters 1-13 and 15: 'Tomas' with consistent accent. Ch14 lines 3764, 3768, 3770, 3900, 3936, 3946: 'Tomas' without accent (if applicable — encoding-dependent). The same chapter uses both forms.
findings.problem: The character name 'Tomas' alternates between accented and unaccented forms within Ch14, and possibly between chapters. This is likely an encoding artifact from the drafting process but creates inconsistency in the published text.
findings.rewrite_direction: Normalize all instances of the name to a single consistent form across the entire manuscript. Use find-and-replace to ensure uniformity.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
